planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Needlepoint Holly is parthenocarpic (self-fertile) and sets fruit without a male pollinator — no separate male plant is required for berry production.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Needlepoint is a refined, upright Chinese holly cultivar valued for two outstanding characteristics: it is one of the most reliably self-fertile hollies (producing bright red berries without requiring a separate male pollinator), and it has narrower, more elegant leaves with a single prominent terminal spine rather than the multiple spines of typical Chinese holly. The reduced spine count makes it significantly more people-friendly than standard Chinese holly (Ilex cornuta) while maintaining excellent deer resistance and the characteristic waxy, deep-green, evergreen foliage. The heavy annual red berry crop is produced reliably from November through winter, providing outstanding wildlife value — berries are consumed by mockingbirds, cedar waxwings, and other fruit-eating birds. The upright, pyramidal to oval habit grows 7–12 feet tall with a somewhat narrower spread, making it suitable for foundation plantings, hedges, and screening in spaces where the spreading Burford holly would be too wide. Needlepoint holly tolerates the full range of Gulf Coast conditions — clay soil, drought, heat, and partial shade — that challenge many landscape shrubs. Apply balanced fertilizer in spring; no regular pruning is needed beyond shaping immediately after the berry season ends in late winter.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; like Burford holly, Dwarf Burford is self-fertile and sets fruit without a male pollinator — a major advantage over hollies requiring cross-pollination.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Dwarf Burford Holly is a compact sport of Burford holly (Ilex cornuta 'Burfordii') that inherits most of the parent's excellent landscape traits — self-fertile heavy red berry production, single-spine leaves, glossy dark-green foliage, and outstanding deer resistance — in a much more restrained package reaching only 3–5 feet tall and wide, compared to the large-shrub to small-tree dimensions of standard Burford holly. The compact size makes Dwarf Burford exceptionally versatile for foundation plantings, low hedges, mass planting as ground cover, and container culture in situations where the full-size Burford would quickly become oversized and require heavy pruning. Like all Chinese hollies, Dwarf Burford produces the characteristic single prominent terminal spine rather than the ring of spines of American holly, making it somewhat less hazardous to plant near walkways and play areas. The heavy red berry crop matures in October–November and persists through winter, providing striking red accent color against the dark-green foliage. Extremely tough and adaptable — tolerates clay, alkaline soil, drought, and heat once established. One of the best small evergreen shrubs for the Gulf Coast region.

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring in moist to dry, acidic to slightly alkaline, well-drained to wet soil in full sun to partial shade; Pendula is a female selection and will produce berries without a separate male plant if other male yaupon hollies are in the vicinity — or plant alongside a male yaupon cultivar to maximize berry production.

directions: Plant in moist to dry, acidic to slightly alkaline, well-drained to wet soil in full sun to partial shade; Weeping Yaupon is a dramatic, architecturally distinctive native holly cultivar with strongly pendulous, cascading branches that create an elegant weeping form quite unlike any other native landscape tree. The long, drooping branches cascade downward from the upright central leader, creating a columnar-weeping silhouette in winter and a dense, cascading curtain of small, glossy, dark-green evergreen leaves during the growing season. In winter when the female plant bears translucent red berries against the dark green foliage along the drooping branches, the display is spectacular. Weeping yaupon is a female Ilex vomitoria selection and requires a nearby male yaupon holly (the species or a male cultivar) within about 100 feet for reliable berry production. Like the species, Weeping Yaupon has extraordinary adaptability — thriving in the full spectrum of Gulf Coast conditions from seasonally flooded clay soils to dry sandy uplands, salt spray, alkaline soils, and both full sun and partial shade. It grows 12–18 feet tall with a cascading, somewhat narrow spread of 6–10 feet. The weeping form makes it outstanding as a specimen, accent, or garden focal point where its distinctive silhouette can be appreciated.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Chinese photinia requires good air circulation — do not plant against walls or in crowded situations where poor air circulation promotes Entomosporium leaf spot disease.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun with good air circulation; Chinese photinia is the large-growing photinia species that serves as the parent of the more commonly planted Fraser photinia (Photinia × fraseri) — a robust, large evergreen shrub or small tree growing 10–25 feet tall with broad, leathery, serrated, glossy dark-green leaves and dramatic new-growth red coloration when young foliage first emerges in spring. The new growth displays the vivid red coloration characteristic of all photinias — a flush of bright red new leaves that contrasts dramatically with the existing dark-green foliage in spring and provides several weeks of striking seasonal color. Chinese photinia is more cold-hardy than Fraser photinia and grows substantially larger, functioning as a true screening tree or large specimen shrub rather than just a foundation shrub. It is notably more resistant to Entomosporium leaf spot disease (the fungal disease that disfigures many Fraser photinias) than its hybrid offspring. White flower clusters in spring are followed by red berries that attract birds. The tall, screening habit makes Chinese photinia valuable for privacy screens and large property divisions — it can reach tree proportions over decades. Provide excellent drainage and good air circulation to minimize leaf spot.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter, spring, or fall in well-drained to dry, slightly acidic to alkaline, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Elaeagnus x ebbingei is extremely adaptable and actually performs best in lean to average soils — avoid planting in rich, heavily amended soil where rank, soft growth is excessive.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, slightly acidic to alkaline, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Ebbinge's Silverberry is a vigorous, large-growing evergreen hybrid elaeagnus — a cross between Elaeagnus macrophylla and E. pungens — producing large, broadly oval, silvery-green to silver-backed leaves on arching, fast-growing stems that create a substantial evergreen screen or windbreak in one to two growing seasons. The large, silvery-underside leaves give the shrub a distinctive, gleaming silver-gray appearance in breezes when the undersides catch the light. In fall, small, creamy-white, intensely sweet-fragrant flowers are produced — remarkably fragrant for small, inconspicuous flowers — followed by small, orange-red berries in spring that are edible (sweet-tart when ripe) and attractive to birds. Like its parent E. pungens, Ebbinge's Elaeagnus is one of the most remarkably tough and adaptable landscape shrubs available: tolerating drought, salt spray, wind, poor soils, alkaline conditions, and coastal exposures that defeat most evergreen shrubs. It grows by nitrogen fixation through root nodules, enriching the surrounding soil. The fast, dense growth and salt tolerance make it outstanding for coastal windbreaks and screening. 'Gilt Edge' and 'Limelight' are named cultivars with bold golden-yellow leaf variegation.
